original_abstract:
On February 6, 2023, Turkey faced one of the most devastating seismic disasters in its history, with two massive earthquakes impacting ten provinces and resulting in catastrophic destruction. In the immediate aftermath, the Ministry of National Education (MoNE) mobilized its vast human resources and manufacturing capacity to provide essential humanitarian aid and restore educational services. This study evaluates the MoNE’s multi-faceted response through the lens of global emergency frameworks, assessing its role in both disaster relief and educational continuity. 

Our findings indicate that MoNE institutions were central to the humanitarian effort, taking a leading role in the production and distribution of food, shelter, and heating supplies. To minimize learning loss, the Ministry rapidly established temporary learning environments—including tents, container classrooms, and prefabricated schools—while deploying teachers and psychological counselors to support affected students. Notable interventions included the establishment of hospital classrooms for injured students and the facilitation of seamless transfers and free boarding for those relocating to other provinces. Drawing on international guidelines from the INEE and UNESCO-IIEP, this study concludes that the MoNE effectively implemented emergency education policies, leveraging organizational expertise gained during the COVID-19 pandemic to mitigate the impact of the disaster.
humanized_abstract:
14 MARCH 2024 ABSTRACT On 6 February 2023, Turkey was hit by two earthquakes of magnitude 6.8 and 7.8 in ten provinces across the country, resulting in massive destruction. Six days after the incident, Ministry of National Education (MoNE) took immediate action by using its enormous potential human resources and production capacity for the provision of humanitarian aid as well as to ensure educational continuity. This research aims to examine the actions of MoNE after the devastating earthquakes that affected the country by means of world emergency response literature and its performance during the emergency and educational processes.

The study found that MoNE was the main institution which provided and supplied foodstuff, sheltering materials and fuel-burning materials. Therefore, the Ministry urgently established temporary schools using tents, containers and prefabricated schools, sent teachers and psychological counselors to the schools for students in order to avoid learning loss. In addition, hospital classrooms were established for the schools of the students injured in the disaster in order to facilitate the transfer of students from the affected provinces to other provinces as well as to provide them with free accommodation. The study relied on the international recommendations and standards referred to in the INEE & UNESCO-IIEP guidebook, pointing out that the role of MoNE in responding to the natural disaster was successful in implementing the emergency educational policies due to the ministry’s competence in responding to the challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic, in addition to its efficient response to the recent earthquake.

document.input:
On February 6, 2023, Turkey faced one of the most devastating seismic disasters in its history, with two massive earthquakes impacting ten provinces and resulting in catastrophic destruction. In the immediate aftermath, the Ministry of National Education (MoNE) mobilized its vast human resources and manufacturing capacity to provide essential humanitarian aid and restore educational services. This study evaluates the MoNE’s multi-faceted response through the lens of global emergency frameworks, assessing its role in both disaster relief and educational continuity. 

Our findings indicate that MoNE institutions were central to the humanitarian effort, taking a leading role in the production and distribution of food, shelter, and heating supplies. To minimize learning loss, the Ministry rapidly established temporary learning environments—including tents, container classrooms, and prefabricated schools—while deploying teachers and psychological counselors to support affected students. Notable interventions included the establishment of hospital classrooms for injured students and the facilitation of seamless transfers and free boarding for those relocating to other provinces. Drawing on international guidelines from the INEE and UNESCO-IIEP, this study concludes that the MoNE effectively implemented emergency education policies, leveraging organizational expertise gained during the COVID-19 pandemic to mitigate the impact of the disaster.
